questions.question_text: Which type of deed provides the GREATEST protection to the buyer in a New York real estate transaction?
distractors: Bargain and Sale deed with covenants
Quitclaim deed with no warranties
Executor's deed from a fiduciary
questions.explanation: A Full Covenant and Warranty deed provides the most protection because it includes all five covenants: seizin, against encumbrances, quiet enjoyment, further assurance, and warranty. This gives the buyer the strongest guarantees of clear title and the grantor's obligation to defend it.

questions.question_text: A homeowner sells her primary residence through a standard residential transaction in New York. She provides a Bargain and Sale deed with covenants against grantor's acts. This deed type:
questions.correct_answer: Warrants only that the grantor has not impaired title
distractors: Provides the same full protection as a Warranty deed
Transfers no warranties or guarantees of any kind
Guarantees quiet enjoyment against all future claims
questions.explanation: A Bargain and Sale deed with covenants against grantor's acts is the most common deed in New York residential transactions. It warrants only that the grantor has done nothing to encumber or impair the title during ownership, but does not guarantee against defects created by prior owners.

questions.question_text: An estate executor is transferring property to the buyer who purchased it at a court-ordered sale. Which deed type would the executor MOST likely deliver?
distractors: Full Covenant and Warranty deed
Bargain and Sale deed with covenants
Quitclaim deed
questions.explanation: An Executor's deed (or Referee's deed in foreclosure) is used when property is conveyed by a fiduciary acting under court authority, such as an estate executor or a referee in a foreclosure sale. These deeds typically carry limited warranties because the fiduciary has no personal knowledge of the title history.

questions.question_text: A divorcing couple agrees that one spouse will transfer her interest to the other. Neither party wants to make any title warranties. Which deed is MOST appropriate?
distractors: Full Covenant and Warranty deed
Bargain and Sale deed with covenants
Referee's deed
questions.explanation: A Quitclaim deed transfers whatever interest the grantor may have without any warranties or guarantees about the quality of title. It is commonly used between family members, divorcing spouses, or to clear title defects where no title guarantees are needed.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives a Bargain and Sale deed WITHOUT covenants. Two years later, she discovers a lien placed by a prior owner. What recourse does the buyer have against her grantor?
questions.correct_answer: None, because the deed carried no warranties
distractors: She can sue under the covenant of seizin
She can demand the grantor clear the lien
She can rescind the sale within three years
questions.explanation: A Bargain and Sale deed without covenants provides minimal protection. It implies the grantor holds title but makes no warranties against encumbrances or defects. Because no covenants were included, the buyer has no contractual claim against the grantor for pre-existing liens.

questions.question_text: For a deed to be eligible for recording in New York, it must include:
questions.correct_answer: The grantor's acknowledgment before a notary
distractors: Signatures of at least two qualified witnesses
Prior approval from the county clerk's office
A certificate of title issued by an attorney
questions.explanation: New York requires that a deed be acknowledged (notarized) before it can be recorded in the county land records. Acknowledgment verifies the identity of the grantor and confirms the deed was signed voluntarily. Recording provides constructive notice to the world of the transfer.

questions.question_text: A grantor signs a deed but places it in her desk drawer, intending to give it to the grantee next month. The grantor dies before delivery. The deed is:
questions.correct_answer: Invalid because delivery and acceptance never occurred
distractors: Valid because the grantor signed it before death
Enforceable if the grantee later finds the deed
Valid upon recording by the estate executor
questions.explanation: A valid deed requires delivery by the grantor and acceptance by the grantee. Without delivery, the deed has no legal effect regardless of signing. Placing a deed in a drawer without surrendering control does not constitute delivery, so the deed is invalid and title does not transfer.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an essential element of a valid deed in New York?
questions.correct_answer: A competent grantor and identifiable grantee
distractors: Recording in the county land records
Payment of the full purchase price
A survey dated within 6 months of closing
questions.explanation: Essential deed elements in New York include a competent grantor, an identifiable grantee, a granting clause, consideration, legal description, habendum clause, grantor's signature, and delivery and acceptance. Recording is required for constructive notice but is not essential for a deed to be valid between the parties.

questions.question_text: Buyer A purchases property and records the deed immediately. Buyer B later purchases the same property from the same seller without knowledge of A's purchase and also records. In New York's recording system, who prevails?
distractors: Buyer B, if B paid a higher price
Buyer B, as the most recent purchaser
Neither, until a court resolves the dispute
questions.explanation: New York follows a race-notice recording statute. A subsequent purchaser is protected only if they both lack notice of the prior conveyance and record first. Since Buyer A recorded first, Buyer B had constructive notice through the public records and cannot claim superior title.

questions.question_text: A property description in a deed references 'Lot 45, Block 12, as shown on the filed map of Sunrise Estates.' This legal description method is known as:
distractors: Metes and bounds description using bearings
Government rectangular survey system
Informal street address description
questions.explanation: A lot and block description references a specific lot and block number on a subdivision plat map filed with the county. This is one of the accepted legal description methods in New York, along with metes and bounds and tax map references.

questions.question_text: In a typical New York residential closing, where does the closing take place?
questions.correct_answer: At the buyer's or lender's attorney office
distractors: At the county courthouse in the clerk's chambers
At the seller's broker's office
At the title insurance company's regional headquarters
questions.explanation: In New York, residential closings typically take place at the office of the buyer's attorney or the lender's attorney. This differs from many other states where closings occur at title companies or escrow offices.

questions.question_text: A title search reveals a 20-year-old unsatisfied judgment lien against a prior owner. The buyer wants protection. What is the BEST solution?
questions.correct_answer: Obtain a title insurance policy covering the defect
distractors: Request a quitclaim deed from the prior property owner
Waive the title defect and accept the financial risk
Record an affidavit disclaiming the judgment lien
questions.explanation: Title insurance protects buyers against losses from covered title defects, including undiscovered liens and judgments. When a title search reveals a potential defect like an old judgment lien, title insurance provides the buyer financial protection if the defect leads to a claim against the property.

questions.question_text: A property owner dies without a will in New York. How is the real property transferred?
questions.correct_answer: By intestate succession through an administrator
distractors: By probate through an executor named by the court
Automatically to the surviving spouse only
Through a referee's deed issued by the county
questions.explanation: When a property owner dies without a will (intestate), the property passes by descent according to New York's intestacy laws. The court appoints an administrator to manage the estate and distribute assets. This differs from testate succession where an executor named in the will handles the estate through probate.

questions.question_text: A person openly occupies and maintains a vacant lot for over 10 years without the owner's permission, paying all property taxes. Under New York law, this person may acquire title through:
distractors: Prescriptive easement only
Lis pendens filing
Equitable conversion
questions.explanation: Adverse possession in New York allows a person to acquire title to property through open, notorious, continuous, hostile, and exclusive possession for the statutory period of 10 years. The claimant must also demonstrate that the possession was under a claim of right.

questions.question_text: A buyer at closing discovers that the contract of sale contained a promise by the seller to repair the roof, which the seller did not fulfill. The deed makes no mention of the repair. Under the merger doctrine, the buyer's claim for roof repair is MOST likely:
questions.correct_answer: Extinguished because the deed merged all prior agreements
distractors: Enforceable under the original contract terms
Preserved because repair promises survive closing
Valid if the buyer files a complaint within 30 days
questions.explanation: The merger doctrine holds that all terms of the contract of sale merge into the deed at closing. Any promises or agreements not included in the deed are generally extinguished. The buyer should have insisted the repair obligation be addressed before closing or included in the deed.
